title: Only valid arguments should be passed to UNIX/POSIX functions
description: What is the potential impact? Many UNIX/POSIX functions put certain constraints on the values of their parameters. The behavior for some of those UNIX/POSIX functions is not defined but instead, their behavior is implementation-defined, if one passes incorrectly constrained parameters. This may lead to undefined behavior depending on a function’s concrete implementation.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

noncompliant: char* ptr = malloc(0); // Noncompliant: zero size allocation
int fd = open(NULL, O_RDONLY); // Noncompliant: NULL pathname
read(-1, buffer, size); // Noncompliant: invalid file descriptor
write(fd, NULL, 10); // Noncompliant: NULL buffer with non-zero count
compliant: char* ptr = malloc(size); // Compliant: proper size allocation
int fd = open("/path/file", O_RDONLY); // Compliant: valid pathname
if (fd >= 0) read(fd, buffer, size); // Compliant: valid file descriptor
if (buffer != NULL) write(fd, buffer, size); // Compliant: validated buffer

checks.message: Only valid arguments should be passed to UNIX/POSIX functions. Invalid arguments detected:
Zero size memory allocations are invalid and may cause undefined behavior.
Negative file descriptors are invalid for read/write operations.
NULL buffer pointer with non-zero count is invalid for write operations.
